meta.title: Sources — Protecting Trade Secrets
meta.description: Principal cases and statutes from Oranburg, Protecting Trade Secrets (1st ed., CAP 2026).

sections.title: Misappropriation defined
sections.text: "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use without consent by a person who used improper means to acquire it, or knew or had reason to know it was derived from improper means.

sections.title: Trade secret defined
sections.text: Information that: (a) Derives independent economic value from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.

sections.title: Injunctive relief
sections.text: Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.

sections.title: Damages
sections.text: Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment not taken into account in computing actual loss.

sections.title: Reasonable royalty
sections.text: If neither damages nor unjust enrichment are provable, the court may order payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited.

sections.title: Attorney's fees
sections.text: If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party.

sections.title: Theft of trade secrets
sections.text: Criminal penalties for theft of trade secrets intended for economic benefit of anyone other than the owner.

sections.title: Whistleblower immunity
sections.text: An individual shall not be held liable for disclosure of a trade secret made in confidence to a government official or attorney solely for reporting a suspected violation of law.

sections.title: Civil remedies
sections.text: Damages for actual loss, unjust enrichment, or in lieu of damages a reasonable royalty for unauthorized disclosure or use.

sections.title: Ex parte seizure
sections.text: Court may order seizure of property to prevent propagation of the trade secret, upon ex parte application under extraordinary circumstances.

sections.title: Extraterritoriality
sections.text: Applies to conduct outside the United States if the offender is a US person or an act in furtherance was committed in the US.

cases.name: Peabody v. Norfolk
cases.rule: Secret know-how is protectable as property. Employees who gain knowledge under a duty of confidence cannot lawfully disclose or profit from it.

cases.name: Vickery v. Welch
cases.rule: A trade secret sold with a business is legally transferable. The seller's obligation to maintain secrecy survives the transaction.

cases.name: Kewanee Oil Co. v. Bicron Corp.
cases.rule: State trade secret law is not preempted by federal patent law. The two systems serve complementary purposes.

cases.name: Coca-Cola Co. v. Coca-Cola Bottling Co.
cases.rule: Coca-Cola's formula qualified as a trade secret — independent economic value from secrecy, access restricted to two employees, formula stored in a bank vault.

cases.name: Motorola Solutions v. Hytera Communications
cases.rule: Systematic theft of proprietary source code violated the DTSA. Court awarded $764.6 million including punitive damages.

cases.name: Compulife Software v. Newman
cases.rule: Giving numerous licensees access without consistent restrictions undermined the trade secret claim. Reasonable efforts require consistent security.

cases.name: Waymo v. Uber Technologies
cases.rule: Proprietary LiDAR designs qualified as trade secrets — significant R&D investment, strict access controls, measurable competitive advantage.

cases.name: Broker Genius v. Zalta
cases.rule: A proprietary pricing algorithm qualified as a trade secret. Operational methods not known outside the business can qualify for protection.

cases.name: Silvaco Data Systems v. Intel Corp.
cases.rule: A well-drafted NDA demonstrates intent and commitment to protection and is a relevant factor in the reasonable efforts analysis.

cases.name: Tewari De-Ox Systems v. Mountain States/Rosen
cases.rule: An overbroad NDA can undermine trade secret claims by failing to define the protected information with precision.

cases.name: Ingersoll-Rand Co. v. Ciavatta
cases.rule: Overly broad invention assignment clauses are unenforceable when they fail to distinguish employer-resource inventions from independent ones.

cases.name: Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP
cases.rule: California noncompetes are void under Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600. Strong public policy favors open competition and employee mobility.

cases.name: Bimbo Bakeries v. Botticella
cases.rule: Executive who accepted a competitor position while accessing confidential files posed a threat justifying injunctive relief. New employer had reason to know the information was obtained improperly.

cases.name: Halliburton v. Axis Technologies
cases.rule: Systematic access controls, password protections, and information segmentation supported the trade secret claim.

cases.name: Auto Channel v. Speedvision Network
cases.rule: Disclosing proprietary concepts during negotiations without an NDA means the information is not protected.

cases.name: Altavion v. Konica Minolta
cases.rule: Technical ideas disclosed in confidence need not be reduced to physical form to be protected. Filing patent applications covering concepts received under confidentiality constitutes misappropriation.

cases.name: C3.ai v. Cummins
cases.rule: After a pilot project ended, launching a similar product using information shared in confidence states a plausible misappropriation claim.

cases.name: Bianco v. Globus Medical
cases.rule: A solo inventor can satisfy reasonable secrecy efforts through documentation and sharing only under confidentiality. Absence of return obligations complicated but did not bar the claim.

cases.name: Accent Packaging v. Leggett & Platt
cases.rule: Reverse engineering is permissible unless an enforceable agreement restricts it. Trade secrets do not shield information obtainable through lawful analysis.

cases.name: GlobeRanger v. Software AG USA
cases.rule: Confidentiality terms in a reseller relationship are insufficient without consistent technical safeguards. Absence of access controls weakened the claim.

cases.name: E.I. du Pont v. Christopher
cases.rule: Aerial surveillance of a plant under construction to reveal a proprietary process constitutes improper means. Companies need only guard against reasonable commercial norms violations.

cases.name: 3M v. Pribyl
cases.rule: Using confidential process information from prior employment subject to ongoing confidentiality obligations constitutes misappropriation, even without taking physical documents.

cases.name: AirFacts v. de Amezaga
cases.rule: Removing confidential information from company control can be actionable. But no signed NDAs, no documented access restrictions, and no internal protocols means the reasonable efforts requirement fails.

cases.name: PepsiCo v. Redmond
cases.rule: Inevitable disclosure doctrine: when a former executive joins a direct competitor in a competing role, injunctive relief is appropriate because the position would almost certainly lead to use of trade secrets.

cases.name: Aday v. Westfield Ins. Co.
cases.rule: A 'claims handling process' described only in general terms lacks specificity. Business acumen is not protectable unless tied to concrete, identifiable information.

cases.name: Religious Technology Center v. Lerma
cases.rule: Once content is published to the internet and circulated publicly, it no longer satisfies the secrecy requirement. Public disclosure destroys trade secret status.

cases.name: Allstate v. Fougere
cases.rule: Inconsistent secrecy policies — sharing some materials widely while protecting others — undermine trade secret claims. Efforts must be clear and uniform.

cases.name: American Registry v. Hanaw
cases.rule: Complaint that fails to describe how information was protected or how conduct was improper — amounting to a complaint about competition — will be dismissed.

cases.name: Mattel v. MGA Entertainment
cases.rule: In the Bratz dolls dispute, the court awarded a reasonable royalty reflecting the licensing value of trade secrets used in developing a competing product.

cases.name: Janssen v. eVenus Pharmaceuticals
cases.rule: Denied ex parte seizure where urgency and imminent risk were not demonstrated. Reinforced narrow application of DTSA seizure.

cases.name: Amnet v. CrossCountry Mortgage
cases.rule: Court awarded attorneys' fees and enhanced damages for willful misappropriation of proprietary software and bad-faith litigation tactics.

cases.name: United States v. Aleynikov
cases.rule: Former Goldman Sachs employee convicted under the Economic Espionage Act for stealing high-frequency trading code. Demonstrated the reach of criminal enforcement.
